title: Introduction to Data Visualisation
summary: Introduces data visualisation — how data becomes an image, visual grammars, symbolic meaning of charts, and data storytelling. Covers the 'Ways of Seeing Data' framework and how narrative structure applies to visualisation.
content_html:
Introduction to Data Visualisation

Data visualisation is the graphical representation of data and information. It is both a technical practice and a communicative act that carries symbolic meaning.

Ways of Seeing Data

Gray et al. (2016) propose a three-stage framework for understanding data visualisation:

  1. World to Data — What information is being represented? What are its sources? How was it generated? Which data sources are combined? How does it prioritise certain things over others?
  2. Data to Image — How is data mediated into graphical form? What graphical techniques were used? What design decisions were taken, and what are their consequences?
  3. Image to Eye — What visual cultures are reflected in the visualisation? Who are its publics? How is it circulated and shared?

Symbolic Meaning of Charts

Charts and graphs carry symbolic meaning beyond their data content. Social semiotics explains this through the concept of semiotic regimes — sign-making is regulated by social practices, authority, expertise, and conformity in particular contexts (Aiello, 2020).

Visual Grammars

A visual grammar is a system of rules for combining visual elements to create meaning. Common examples include:

  • Bar charts — compare single values across categories
  • Pie charts — communicate segments of a whole
  • Timelines — show information over time
  • Choropleth maps — colour-coded maps for geographical data
  • Bold headings and icons — emphasis and hierarchy

Cultural and social histories shape the meaning of visual elements like colour — these histories are traced over time to understand how meaning is made and changed (Aiello, 2020).

Data Stories

Telling stories with data involves three key questions:

  1. What does the data say?
  2. What message do you want to convey?
  3. What visual structure, layout, and hierarchy can tell it?

Data stories can follow basic narrative structures (beginning–middle–end) or more complex non-linear narratives. The narrative structure determines how the viewer experiences and interprets the data.

exercises.title: Ways of Seeing Data
exercises.prompt: In Gray et al.'s (2016) framework, which stage asks 'How are the data mediated into graphical form?'

exercises.title: Chart Types
exercises.prompt: Which chart type is most appropriate for communicating segments of a whole?

exercises.title: Visual Grammar
exercises.prompt: What is a 'visual grammar' in the context of data visualisation?
options: A system of rules for combining visual elements to create meaning
A list of approved fonts and colours for charts
The written caption that accompanies a visualisation
A guide to choosing the correct chart type
exercises.answer: A system of rules for combining visual elements to create meaning

exercises.title: Data Storytelling
exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the key questions in telling stories with data?
options: What message do you want to convey?
What visual structure can tell it?
